concepts.title: Halogenation
concepts.conceptBody:
Chemical Reactions of Alkanes

  • Alkanes are generally inert at room temperature as they have not any functional group
  • They undergo free radical substitution reaction

Radical substitution Mechanism

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{Cl}+\text{HCI}$

The organic product is chloromthane.

One of the hydrogen atoms in the methane has been replaced by a chlorine atom, so this is substitution reaction. However, the reaction doesn’t stop there, and all the hydrogens in the methane can in turn be replaced by chlorine atom.

The mechanism

The mechanism involves a chain reaction. During a chain reaction, for every reactive species you start off with, a new one is generated at the end - and this keeps the process going.

The overall process is known as free radical substitution, or as a free radical chain reaction.

Chain initiation

The chain is initiated (started) by UV light breaking a chlorine molecule into free radicals

$\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\to 2\text{Cl}\centerdot $

Chain propagation reactions

These are the reactions which keep the chain going.

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}+\text{Cl}\bullet \to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\bullet \text{HCl}$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\bullet +\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CI}+\text{Cl}\bullet $

Chain termination reactions

These are reactions which remove free radicals from the system without replacing then by new ones.

$2\text{Cl}\bullet \to \text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\bullet +\text{Cl}\bullet \to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{Cl}$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\bullet +\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$

The potential energy curve for the halogenation (chlorination) of alkane is shown as

concepts.title: Factors determining rate of free radical substitution
concepts.conceptBody:
Factors determining rate of free radical substitution

In more complex alkanes, the abstraction of each different kind of hydrogen atom gives a different isomeric product. Three factors determine the relative yields of isomeric product.

1. Probability Factor: This factor is based on the number of each kind of hydrogen atoms in the alkane molecule. For example, in $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ there are six equivalent $1\text{H}$ and four equivalent $2\text{H}$. The probability of abstracting a $1\text{H}$ to $2\text{H}$ is 6 to 4 , or 3 to 2 .

2. Reactivity of ${{\mathbf{H}}^{\bullet }}$: The order of reactivity of hydrogen atoms is $3>2>1$.

3. Reactivity of ${{\mathbf{X}}^{\bullet }}$: The more reactive $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{\bullet }}$ is less selective and more influenced by the probability factor. The less reactive $\text{Br}\bullet $ is more selective and less influenced by the probability factor, as summarized by the Reactivity-Selectivity Principle. If the attacking species is more reactive, it will be less selective and the yields will be determined by the probability factor as well as reactivity of hydrogen atoms while if the species attacking is less reactive and more selective, the yield of the product is governed exclusively by reactivity of hydrogen atoms.

$\underset{\text{n-butane}}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}}}\,\xrightarrow[{{25}^{{}^\circ }}\text{C}]{\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}/\text{hv}}\underset{\text{ (28 }\!\!%\!\!\text{ ) }}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}(\text{Cl})}}\,+\underset{(72%)}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{CH}(\text{Cl})\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}}}\,$

$\underset{\text{isobutane}}{\mathop{{{(C{{H}_{3}})}_{2}}CHC{{H}_{3}}}}\,\xrightarrow[25{}^\circ C]{C{{l}_{2}}/h\upsilon }\underset{(64%)}{\mathop{{{(C{{H}_{3}})}_{2}}CHC{{H}_{2}}Cl}}\,+\underset{\text{(36 }\!\!%\!\!\text{ )}}{\mathop{{{(C{{H}_{3}})}_{3}}C-Cl}}\,$

$\underset{\text{n-butane}}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}}}\,\xrightarrow[127{}^\circ C]{B{{r}_{2}}/h\upsilon }\underset{\text{(2 }\!\!%\!\!\text{ )}}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Br}}}\,+\underset{(98%)}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{CH}(\text{Br})\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}}}\,$

$\underset{\text{isobutane}}{\mathop{{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\text{CHC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}}}\,\xrightarrow[127{}^\circ C]{B{{r}_{2}}/h\upsilon }\underset{\text{traces}}{\mathop{{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\text{CHC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Br}}}\,+\underset{(\text{over 99 }\!\!%\!\!\text{ })}{\mathop{{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{3}}\text{C}-\text{Br}}}\,$

In the chlorination of isobutane abstraction of one of the nine primary hydrogens leads to the formation of isobutyl chlorides, whereas abstraction of a single tertiary hydrogen leads to the formation of tert-butyl chloride. The probability favours formation of isobutyl chloride by the ratio of 9: 1. But the experimental results show the ratio roughly to be 2: 1 or 9: 4.5. Evidently, about 4.5 times as many collisions with the tertiary hydrogen are successful as collisions with the primary hydrogens. The ${{\text{E}}_{\text{act }}}$ is less for abstraction of a tertiary hydrogen than for the abstraction of a primary hydrogen.

The rate of abstraction of hydrogen atoms is always found to follow the sequence $3>2>1$. At room temperature $(25\text{C})$, the relative rates in chlorination are 5.0: 3.8: 1.0 respectively for 3,2 and 1 hydrogen atoms. Using these values, we can predict quite well the ratio of isomeric chlorination products from a given alkane. For example,

\[\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\xrightarrow[\text{light, }{{25}^{{}^\circ }}\text{C}]{\text{Cl}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Cl}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{CHClC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\]

$\frac{\text{n}-\text{ butyl chloride }}{\text{ sec }-\text{ butyl chloride }}=\frac{\text{ number of }{{1}^{{}^\circ }}\text{H}}{\text{ number of }{{2}^{{}^\circ }}\text{H}}\times \frac{\text{ reactivity of }{{1}^{{}^\circ }}\text{H}}{\text{ reactivity of }{{2}^{{}^\circ }}\text{H}}=\frac{6}{4}\times \frac{1.0}{3.8}=\frac{3}{7.6}\text{ equivalent to }\frac{28%}{72%}$

Inspite of these difference in reactivity, chlorination rarely yields a great excess of any single isomer. In most cases, both the products are formed in considerable amounts.

The same sequence of reactivity, $3>2>1$, is found in bromination, but with enormously larger reactivity ratios. At $127\text{C}$ the relative rates per hydrogen atom in bromination are 1600: 82: 1 respectively for 3,2 and 1 hydrogen atoms. Here, differences in reactivity are so marked that it outweights probability factor. Hence bromination almost exclusively gives selective product. In bromination of isobutane at $127\text{C}$,

$\frac{\text{ isobutyl bromide }}{\text{ tert-butyl bromide }}=\frac{\text{ number of }{{1}^{{}^\circ }}\text{H}}{\text{ number of }{{3}^{{}^\circ }}\text{H}}\times \frac{\text{ reactivity of }{{1}^{{}^\circ }}\text{H}}{\text{ reactivity of }{{3}^{{}^\circ }}\text{H}}$

$=\frac{9}{1}\times \frac{1}{1600}=\frac{9}{1600}\text{ equivalent to }\frac{0.5%}{99.5%}$

Hence, tert-butyl bromide happens to be the exclusive product (over 99%) with traces of isobutyl bromide.

The reason for the higher selectivity in bromination as compared to chlorination is due to the following explanation.

According to the general principle, for comparable reactions, the more endothermic (or less exothermic) reaction has a transition state (TS), which more closely resembles the intermediate and may more closely resemble the ground state (reactants). Since attack by $Br\centerdot $ on alkane is more endothermic than attack by $\text{Cl}\centerdot $, its TS shows more $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond breaking and more $\text{H}-\text{Br}$ bond formation. Any stabilization in the intermediate radical also occurs in the corresponding TS. Therefore, a TS leading to a $3{{\text{R}}^{\centerdot }}$, has a lower enthalpy than one leading to a $2{{\text{R}}^{\centerdot }}$, which in turn has a lower enthalpy than one leading to $1{{\text{R}}^{\centerdot }}$, the relative rates of $\text{H}$-abstraction by $\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{\centerdot }}$ are $3>2>1$. The TS for $\text{H}-$ abstraction by $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{\bullet }}$ has less $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond breaking and less $\text{H}-\text{Cl}$ bond formation. The nature of the incipient radical has less effect on the enthalpy of the TS and on the rate of its formation. Hence there is less difference in the rate of formation of the three kinds of ${{R}^{{}^\circ }}$ s. In the attack by the comparatively unreactive bromine atom, the transition state is reached late in the reaction process, after the alkyl group has developed considerable radical character. In the attack by the highly reactive chlorine atom, the transition state is reached early, when the alkyl group has gained very little radical character. Thus bromination is more selective than chlorination.

  • Bromination is selective for the $\text{R}-\text{H}$ that gives the most stable radical
  • Chlorination is less selective.
  • The termination steps are low probability due to the low concentration of the radical species meaning that the chances of them colliding is very low.

Radical chain mechanism for reaction of methane with $\mathbf{B}{{\mathbf{r}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$

concepts.title: Free radical substitution of aromatic hydrocarbons
Oxidation, Sulphonation and nitration of alkanes
concepts.conceptBody:
Oxidation, Sulphonation and nitration of alkanes

All alkanes readily burn in excess of air or oxygen to form carbon dioxide and water.

${{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{H}}_{2\text{n}+2}}+\frac{(3\text{n}+1)}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\to \text{nC}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+\left( \frac{2\text{n}+2}{2} \right){{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}(\text{l})$

On the other hand, controlled oxidation under various condition, leads to different products. Extensive oxidation gives a mixture of products in which no chain fission has occurred. Under moderate conditions mixed ketones are the major products and oxidation in the presence of boric acid produces a mixture of secondary alcohols. The oxidation of alkanes in the vapour state occurs via free radicals, e.g. alkyl $(\text{R}\bullet )$, alkylperoxy $(\text{ROO}\bullet )$ and alkoxy $(\text{RO}\bullet )$. Oxidising reagents such as potassium permaganate readily oxidise a tertiary hydrogen atom to a hydroxyl group. For example, isobutane is oxidised to t-butanol.

${{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{3}}\text{CH}+[\text{O}]\xrightarrow[{}]{KMn{{O}_{4}}}{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{3}}\text{COH}$

2. Sulphonation

It is the process of replacing hydrogen atom by a sulphonic acid group, $\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\text{H}$. Sulphonation of a normal alkane from hexane onwards may be carried out by treating the alkane with oleum (fuming sulphuric acid). The order of case of replacement of $\text{H}$-atoms in tertiary. Compounds is very much easier than secondary compounds replacement of $\text{H}$-atoms by sulphonic is very slow indeed. Isobutane, which contains a tertiary hydrogen atom, is readily sulphonated to give t-butyl sulphonic acid.

${{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{3}}\text{CH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}/\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\to {{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{3}}\text{CS}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\text{H}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

3. Nitration

Under certain conditions alkanes react with nitric acid, when a hydrogen atom will be replaced by a nitro-group, \[N{{O}_{2}}.\] This process is known as nitration. Nitration of the alkanes may be carried out in the vapour phase between 150 and $475\text{C}$, when a complex mixture of mononitroalkanes is obtaines. The mixture consists of all the possible mononitroderivatives and the nitro-compounds formed by every possibility of chain fission of the alkane. For example, propane gives a mixture of 1-nitropropane, 2-nitropropane, nitroethane and nitromethane.
